acceptance_targets: [PROSE-04-02] The revised chapter contains no more than 4 instances of 'which was' or 'which meant' as a parenthetical qualifier. The double-'which meant' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 is eliminated.

[PROSE-04-03] The revised chapter contains no more than 3 instances of 'a woman who' or 'a woman [verb]-ing' as an appositive characterization formula. No two instances appear within 10 lines of each other. The double use at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:23 is reduced to one or zero.

[PROSE-04-04] The revised chapter contains at least 3 sentences of 15 words or fewer at emotional peaks, creating rhythmic contrast with the surrounding long constructions. At least one occurs in the father-telling section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:57-85) and at least one in the being-known realization (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:53-55 or 73-77). These short sentences should feel like compression, not clipping — grammatically complete and emotionally weighted.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 4 instances of 'which was' or 'which meant' as a parenthetical qualifier. The double-'which meant' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 is eliminated.
findings.evidence: "which was" and "which meant" parenthetical constructions appear 9 times: "which was funnier and more precise" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:3), "which was a reshuffling of priorities she chose not to examine" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:9), "which meant he texted late, which meant she woke" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11, two instances in one sentence), "which was the most she'd written about anything in months" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:19), "which was itself a kind of answer" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:31), "which was twelve words on a screen from a man in Oregon" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:53), "which was the problem" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:59).
findings.problem: The "which was" parenthetical has become the chapter's default mechanism for appending qualification, irony, or contextual detail to sentences. While some instances are effective (line 31's 'itself a kind of answer' has genuine wit), the construction's frequency creates a predictable sentence rhythm: statement, then 'which was' hedge or expansion. This is a subtler tic than the 'the way' construction but compounds the sense of every sentence following the same digestive pattern.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce to no more than 4 instances. Targets for replacement: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:9 — restructure as a new sentence or em-dash appositive (e.g., 'she'd rearranged her home screen to put it where Instagram used to be — a reshuffling of priorities she chose not to examine'). snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 — the double 'which meant' in one sentence is the clearest tic; restructure so the causal chain doesn't rely on repeating the same connective. snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:53 — 'which was twelve words on a screen' can be folded into the sentence differently (e.g., parenthetical dash or a separate clause). Keep strong instances like line 31 and line 59 where the construction carries real rhetorical weight.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 3 instances of 'a woman who' or 'a woman [verb]-ing' as an appositive characterization formula. No two instances appear within 10 lines of each other. The double use at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:23 is reduced to one or zero.
findings.evidence: "a woman who" (or "a woman [verb]-ing") appositive construction appears 8 times: "a woman who knew exactly how much wanting she could safely reveal" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11), "a woman who had just committed a sentence to the permanent record" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:17), "a woman who lived alone" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:23, used twice in the same sentence), "a woman who had better things to do" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:89), "a woman building a case" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:93), "a woman who had been as hopeful as Nora" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:131), "a woman closing a topic she intended to reopen" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:141).
findings.problem: This construction generalizes specific characters into archetypal types. When Nora is described as 'a woman who knew exactly how much wanting she could safely reveal,' the prose distances the reader from Nora's specificity and renders her as a category. When Margo eats her apple with the precision of 'a woman building a case,' the formula treats character as a template rather than an individual. At 8 instances, the construction transforms individual moments into a repeating pattern of woman-as-archetype that paradoxically makes the characters feel less particular.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce to no more than 3 instances across the chapter. Replace the rest with constructions that stay closer to the specific character rather than reaching for the generic. Targets: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 — instead of 'a woman who knew exactly how much wanting she could safely reveal,' stay in Nora's specific consciousness (e.g., 'she monitored the temperature of her reply with the same precision she brought to — '). snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:23 — the double use in one sentence ('a woman who lived alone... a woman who lived alone with three hundred books') is the most conspicuous; restructure so the comparison doesn't rely on repeating the formula. snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:89 and 93 — Margo gets two 'a woman who/building' constructions within five lines; replace at least one with a description that names Margo or uses a pronoun. Keep the strongest instance (line 131's 'a woman who had been as hopeful as Nora' is effective because the comparison is specific).

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains at least 3 sentences of 15 words or fewer at emotional peaks, creating rhythmic contrast with the surrounding long constructions. At least one occurs in the father-telling section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:57-85) and at least one in the being-known realization (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:53-55 or 73-77). These short sentences should feel like compression, not clipping — grammatically complete and emotionally weighted.
findings.evidence: The chapter's first 86 lines consist overwhelmingly of long compound sentences (50-150+ words) built through em-dashes, parenthetical qualifications, and trailing clauses. Representative: the opening sentence (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:3) runs approximately 150 words with four subordinate clauses. Line 7 is a single sentence of approximately 120 words. Lines 11, 15, 17 follow the same pattern. The emotional peaks — the being-known realization (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:75) and the father-telling climax (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:73) — use the same long, qualified sentence rhythm as transitional narration about weather and subway rides.
findings.problem: The style bible's rhythm target calls for compression at emotional peaks: 'Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision.' The chapter delivers its big moments — Caleb's response about the father, Nora's sensation of being known — in the same extended, digressive sentence shape used for establishing the daily texture of texting. When everything runs at the same rhythmic frequency, nothing stands apart. The reader cannot feel which moments are the chapter's peaks because the prose treats every observation with the same expansive, qualifying attention. The style bible also warns: 'Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause.' This is true, but the chapter has the opposite problem: every moment, important or not, is a long sentence.
findings.rewrite_direction: Introduce 3-4 short, compressed sentences (under 15 words) at the chapter's emotional peaks to create rhythmic contrast. Target locations: (1) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:53 — the moment Nora presses the phone to her sternum; consider a short declarative sentence that interrupts the long surrounding construction. (2) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:75 — the seven-times-reading moment; let one of the readings or the subsequent sensation land in a compressed sentence rather than the long, qualified construction that currently carries it. (3) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:69 — after Nora decides to leave the message about her father; a short sentence here would mark the significance of the choice. (4) The father-telling section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:57-85) needs at least one moment of silence or compression to counterbalance the sustained long-sentence momentum.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Focus on prose and copyedit finish: diction precision, rhythm control, sentence-level clarity, grammar, line-level polish without flattening style, and explicit aesthetic-risk audit (sanitization is the primary risk — do not soften or euphemize; push toward creative risk and specificity).
revision_pass.label: prose/copyedit fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
